item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered no-voting just because the rating was at +123 and I found that aesthetically pleasing. Alas, the ending was too funny (and I just love puns).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the read, but I’m leaving it at a no-vote with the lack of object class, and no solid (to me) reason to not include it.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
What do you mean?

Edit: Wait are you saying that an article has to be special before removing an object class? An object class would communicate nothing of value to the audience in this article, so why would I include it?
Not nearly enough done with the piece to justify the omission of the object class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An SCP born out of a pun. Who knew something like this would actually fit in universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Olmos' reaction at the end is really what makes this article for me and I feel it could even be expanded on. Though the article does currently do a good job of not badgering the reader on the head with it.
The pun is also quite fun.

+ Minor nitpick
– hide block

SCP-4613 sufferers

To me "sufferer" feels a bit dramatic for a clinical document. Perhaps victim, subject, or affected subject would be more appropriate? Might just be a personal preference though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how Olmos both expects the result of his appearance and isn't aware he is the potential cause. That and the Bladerunner reference in the MTF.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so goddamn stupid yet you made it work. I especially love the ending. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's funny. I checked to see if it was a -J, and it's not. I'm not sure if I like this as a main list article, as I second Henzoid's concerns. From a distance, this anomaly seems more like an internet meme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does not have one. I've been flirting with the idea of leaving it out for a while now, and have done so here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just the right amount of stupid for me, done in the proper tone. I like it: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, (I've written about the interaction between celebrity and the anomalous a couple of times myself) and it definitely leaves some fun questions on the table, but honestly you already had me when I saw the title.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I briefly considered this, but then I thought about how it has actual effects on the memory of attendees (at least inasmuch as they leave before the event concludes) and that social media data would show these "hoaxes" occurring far more often for one individual than anyone else.

That's more than enough to pique Foundation interest and get them to investigate more, and once they do it's not a huge leap to notice that people are having their memories affected.

It was a concern of mine going in, and I very much appreciate the feedback, but I don't know that it's as serious a problem as you think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like the concept of anomalous celebrity hype, but I'm novoting for the reason that despite its strange nature, before the test, SCP-4613 wouldn't have been identifiable as an anomaly. What you describe is out of place, but not nearly impossible human behavior, and while they find out later that it is in fact more than it first seems, I'm not convinced that the Foundation would even try doing tests on this unless there was more to the base anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It's a shame they won't live. But then again who does?"

Image is by Gage Skidmore and can be found here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Edward_James_Olmos_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg

Special thanks to The Great Hippo for helping me clean up some errors after I posted. Much appreciated. Check out their other works here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/the-great-hippo
